problem text: Consider two independent random variables X and Y, each drawn from a standard normal distribution. What is the likelihood that both X and X+Y are simultaneously greater than zero?
problem solution: We want to determine $$P(X>0, X+Y>0)$$ where X and Y are independent $$N(0,1)$$ random variables. First, note that $$X+Y>0$$ implies $$Y>-X$$. Geometrically, the point $$(X,Y)$$ lies in the two-dimensional plane with circular symmetry (each direction is equally likely), because X and Y are independent standard normals. We translate the condition $$X>0$$ into the right half of the plane, and the condition $$Y>-X$$ into the region above the line $$Y=-X$$. The angle subtended by these conditions can be analyzed in polar coordinates, or by a symmetry argument. Specifically: 1) $$X>0$$ restricts us to angles where $$\cos(\theta)>0$$, and 2) $$Y>-X$$ restricts us to angles above the line $$\theta=\frac{3\pi}{4}$$ or below the line $$\theta=-\frac{\pi}{4}$$ (when measured from the positive X-axis). The intersection of these angular regions is a wedge that spans a total angle of $$\frac{3\pi}{4}$$ out of the full circle of $$2\pi$$. Hence, since the distribution is rotationally invariant, the probability is the ratio of these angles: $$\frac{3\pi/4}{2\pi} = \frac{3}{8}.$$ Therefore, $$\displaystyle P(X>0, X+Y>0) = \frac{3}{8}.$$
problem hint: Convert to polar coordinates or draw the lines X=0 and Y=-X. Analyze the sector of angles that satisfies both conditions.
